Src

UniProt ID: P05480
Organism: Mus musculus
Review Status: COMPLETE
📝 Provide Detailed Feedback

Gene Description

SRC is a membrane-associated non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase with SH3, SH2, and catalytic kinase domains. It is recruited to activated receptors, adhesion complexes, and cytoskeletal scaffolds where it phosphorylates tyrosine residues to regulate intracellular signaling, adhesion, migration, osteoclast function, epithelial responses, and cell growth contexts.

Reason: SRC is a soluble non-receptor kinase recruited to cytosolic, membrane, junctional, caveolar, and focal-adhesion signaling assemblies.

GO:0004715 non-membrane spanning protein tyrosine kinase activity

Reason: SRC is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase; tyrosine phosphorylation of protein substrates is its primary molecular activity.

file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-bioreason.md
A cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase that uses an SH3-SH2-kinase architecture to assemble with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners

Summary: Direct SRC tyrosine phosphorylation activity
Reason: This term directly describes the catalytic output of SRC kinase activity on protein tyrosine residues.

Core Functions

SRC phosphorylates protein tyrosine residues as a regulated non-receptor kinase assembled with receptor, adaptor, and cytoskeletal signaling complexes through SH2 and SH3 domain interactions.

Molecular Function:
non-membrane spanning protein tyrosine kinase activity
Directly Involved In:
peptidyl-tyrosine phosphorylation intracellular signal transduction
Cellular Locations:
cytosol plasma membrane focal adhesion
Supporting Evidence:
  • file:mouse/Src/Src-notes.md
    SRC is a non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase with SH3-SH2-kinase architecture.
  • file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-falcon.md
    Falcon synthesis supports Src as a regulated non-receptor tyrosine kinase that phosphorylates protein substrates in receptor, adhesion, and cytoskeletal signaling complexes.

📚 Additional Documentation

Deep Research Bioreason

(Src-deep-research-bioreason.md)

BioReason Chat Export

Exported on March 22, 2026 at 01:27 AM

Organism: Mus musculus

Sequence:

Thinking Trace

I begin by dissecting the InterPro domain architecture and its order along the sequence. The N-terminal third contains IPR036028 (SH3-like domain superfamily, residues 63–155) with a well-defined IPR001452 SH3 domain core from residues 83–144. Immediately downstream lies IPR036860 (SH2 domain superfamily, residues 140–289) and a precise IPR000980 SH2 domain from residues 148–247. This tandem SH3–SH2 arrangement is a hallmark of non-receptor tyrosine kinases that couple modular recognition of proline-rich ligands (via SH3) and phosphotyrosine motifs (via SH2) to regulate catalytic output. The C-terminal half is dominated by the kinase engine: IPR011009 (Protein kinase-like domain superfamily, residues 258–528) houses the bilobal catalytic core that is specifically annotated as a tyrosine kinase by IPR020635 (Tyrosine-protein kinase, catalytic domain, residues 269–518), IPR000719 (Protein kinase domain, residues 269–522), and IPR001245 (Serine-threonine/tyrosine-protein kinase, catalytic domain, residues 270–517). Within this core, IPR017441 marks the ATP-binding site (residues 275–297), and IPR008266 annotates the tyrosine-kinase active site (residues 384–396). The overall family classification IPR050198 (Non-receptor tyrosine kinases involved in cell signaling, residues 89–530) ties these modules together into a cytosolic signaling enzyme. The linear architecture—N-terminal SH3 followed by SH2 and then the C-terminal catalytic domain—creates a regulated switch: SH3/SH2 dictate docking and allosteric control, while the kinase core executes phosphorylation.

This modular layout dictates molecular function. The ATP-binding pocket (IPR017441) and active-site signature (IPR008266) specify phosphotransfer chemistry on tyrosine residues, establishing protein tyrosine kinase activity and ATP binding as core activities. The SH3 and SH2 domains cause high-affinity, context-dependent assembly with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners, thereby gating catalytic access and substrate selection. Thus, the primary molecular functions are protein tyrosine kinase activity and ATP binding.

From function to process, tyrosine phosphorylation cascades propagate and modulate intracellular pathways. The presence of a non-receptor tyrosine kinase core (IPR050198) and the SH3–SH2 regulatory modules couple extracellular and intracellular cues to phosphorylation-dependent signaling hubs. This architecture drives intracellular signal transduction and supports cell survival and division programs; therefore, it feeds into broader cell signaling and cell proliferation pathways.

Cellular location follows from the absence of transmembrane segments and the canonical behavior of non-receptor tyrosine kinases. The SH3–SH2–kinase assembly is soluble and recruited to membranes and cytoskeletal scaffolds via domain-mediated interactions, but its basal compartment is the cytoplasm. This is consistent with a cytoplasmic localization where signaling complexes assemble and remodel.

Mechanistically, the SH3 domain (IPR001452, residues 83–144) binds proline-rich segments to position the enzyme near actin and adaptor scaffolds; the SH2 domain (IPR000980, residues 148–247) recognizes phosphotyrosine motifs on activated receptors or adaptors, stabilizing the active conformation of the catalytic core. The catalytic region (IPR020635/IPR000719/IPR001245; residues ~269–522) then transfers phosphate from ATP to tyrosine residues on local substrates, amplifying downstream signaling. I hypothesize that this kinase transiently assembles with cytoplasmic adaptors and actin-associated complexes, tuning phosphorylation flux that supports survival and proliferation signals emanating from growth factor and integrin pathways.

Functional Summary

A cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase that uses an SH3–SH2–kinase architecture to assemble with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners and phosphorylate tyrosine residues on nearby substrates. By coupling modular docking to catalysis, it propagates intracellular signaling pathways that promote survival and cell division. Its soluble nature and scaffold-directed recruitment position it within cytoplasmic signaling assemblies where ATP-fueled phosphorylation drives downstream pathway activation.

UniProt Summary

Tyrosine-protein kinase involved in the homing and function of B-cells and the regulation of cell survival and cell division.

InterPro Domains

  • SH3-like domain superfamily (IPR036028, homologous_superfamily) — residues 63-155
  • SH3 domain (IPR001452, domain) — residues 83-144
  • Non-receptor tyrosine kinases involved in cell signaling (IPR050198, family) — residues 89-530
  • SH2 domain superfamily (IPR036860, homologous_superfamily) — residues 140-289
  • SH2 domain (IPR000980, domain) — residues 148-247
  • Protein kinase-like domain superfamily (IPR011009, homologous_superfamily) — residues 258-528
  • Tyrosine-protein kinase, catalytic domain (IPR020635, domain) — residues 269-518
  • Protein kinase domain (IPR000719, domain) — residues 269-522

  • Protein kinase, ATP binding site (IPR017441, binding_site) — residues 275-297
  • Tyrosine-protein kinase, active site (IPR008266, active_site) — residues 384-396

Organism: Mus musculus

Thinking Trace

I begin by dissecting the InterPro domain architecture and its order along the sequence. The N-terminal third contains IPR036028 (SH3-like domain superfamily, residues 63–155) with a well-defined IPR001452 SH3 domain core from residues 83–144. Immediately downstream lies IPR036860 (SH2 domain superfamily, residues 140–289) and a precise IPR000980 SH2 domain from residues 148–247. This tandem SH3–SH2 arrangement is a hallmark of non-receptor tyrosine kinases that couple modular recognition of proline-rich ligands (via SH3) and phosphotyrosine motifs (via SH2) to regulate catalytic output. The C-terminal half is dominated by the kinase engine: IPR011009 (Protein kinase-like domain superfamily, residues 258–528) houses the bilobal catalytic core that is specifically annotated as a tyrosine kinase by IPR020635 (Tyrosine-protein kinase, catalytic domain, residues 269–518), IPR000719 (Protein kinase domain, residues 269–522), and IPR001245 (Serine-threonine/tyrosine-protein kinase, catalytic domain, residues 270–517). Within this core, IPR017441 marks the ATP-binding site (residues 275–297), and IPR008266 annotates the tyrosine-kinase active site (residues 384–396). The overall family classification IPR050198 (Non-receptor tyrosine kinases involved in cell signaling, residues 89–530) ties these modules together into a cytosolic signaling enzyme. The linear architecture—N-terminal SH3 followed by SH2 and then the C-terminal catalytic domain—creates a regulated switch: SH3/SH2 dictate docking and allosteric control, while the kinase core executes phosphorylation.

This modular layout dictates molecular function. The ATP-binding pocket (IPR017441) and active-site signature (IPR008266) specify phosphotransfer chemistry on tyrosine residues, establishing protein tyrosine kinase activity and ATP binding as core activities. The SH3 and SH2 domains cause high-affinity, context-dependent assembly with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners, thereby gating catalytic access and substrate selection. Thus, the primary molecular functions are protein tyrosine kinase activity and ATP binding.

From function to process, tyrosine phosphorylation cascades propagate and modulate intracellular pathways. The presence of a non-receptor tyrosine kinase core (IPR050198) and the SH3–SH2 regulatory modules couple extracellular and intracellular cues to phosphorylation-dependent signaling hubs. This architecture drives intracellular signal transduction and supports cell survival and division programs; therefore, it feeds into broader cell signaling and cell proliferation pathways.

Cellular location follows from the absence of transmembrane segments and the canonical behavior of non-receptor tyrosine kinases. The SH3–SH2–kinase assembly is soluble and recruited to membranes and cytoskeletal scaffolds via domain-mediated interactions, but its basal compartment is the cytoplasm. This is consistent with a cytoplasmic localization where signaling complexes assemble and remodel.

Mechanistically, the SH3 domain (IPR001452, residues 83–144) binds proline-rich segments to position the enzyme near actin and adaptor scaffolds; the SH2 domain (IPR000980, residues 148–247) recognizes phosphotyrosine motifs on activated receptors or adaptors, stabilizing the active conformation of the catalytic core. The catalytic region (IPR020635/IPR000719/IPR001245; residues ~269–522) then transfers phosphate from ATP to tyrosine residues on local substrates, amplifying downstream signaling. I hypothesize that this kinase transiently assembles with cytoplasmic adaptors and actin-associated complexes, tuning phosphorylation flux that supports survival and proliferation signals emanating from growth factor and integrin pathways.

Functional Summary

A cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase that uses an SH3–SH2–kinase architecture to assemble with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners and phosphorylate tyrosine residues on nearby substrates. By coupling modular docking to catalysis, it propagates intracellular signaling pathways that promote survival and cell division. Its soluble nature and scaffold-directed recruitment position it within cytoplasmic signaling assemblies where ATP-fueled phosphorylation drives downstream pathway activation.

UniProt Summary

Tyrosine-protein kinase involved in the homing and function of B-cells and the regulation of cell survival and cell division.

  • SH3 domain (IPR001452, domain) — residues 83-144
  • Non-receptor tyrosine kinases involved in cell signaling (IPR050198, family) — residues 89-530
  • SH2 domain superfamily (IPR036860, homologous_superfamily) — residues 140-289

Gene Research for Functional Annotation

⚠️ CRITICAL: Gene/Protein Identification Context

BEFORE YOU BEGIN RESEARCH: You MUST verify you are researching the CORRECT gene/protein. Gene symbols can be ambiguous, especially for less well-characterized genes from non-model organisms.

Target Gene/Protein Identity (from UniProt):

  • UniProt Accession: P05480
  • Protein Description: RecName: Full=Proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase Src {ECO:0000305}; EC=2.7.10.2 {ECO:0000269|PubMed:8910389}; AltName: Full=Proto-oncogene c-Src; AltName: Full=pp60c-src; Short=p60-Src;
  • Gene Information: Name=Src {ECO:0000312|MGI:MGI:98397};
  • Organism (full): Mus musculus (Mouse).
  • Protein Family: Belongs to the protein kinase superfamily. Tyr protein
  • Key Domains: Kinase-like_dom_sf. (IPR011009); Non-receptor_tyrosine_kinases. (IPR050198); Prot_kinase_dom. (IPR000719); Protein_kinase_ATP_BS. (IPR017441); Ser-Thr/Tyr_kinase_cat_dom. (IPR001245)

MANDATORY VERIFICATION STEPS:

  1. Check if the gene symbol "Src" matches the protein description above
  2. Verify the organism is correct: Mus musculus (Mouse).
  3. Check if protein family/domains align with what you find in literature
  4. If you find literature for a DIFFERENT gene with the same or similar symbol, STOP

If Gene Symbol is Ambiguous or You Cannot Find Relevant Literature:

DO NOT PROCEED WITH RESEARCH ON A DIFFERENT GENE. Instead:
- State clearly: "The gene symbol 'Src' is ambiguous or literature is limited for this specific protein"
- Explain what you found (e.g., "Found extensive literature on a different gene with the same symbol in a different organism")
- Describe the protein based ONLY on the UniProt information provided above
- Suggest that the protein function can be inferred from domain/family information

Research Target:

Please provide a comprehensive research report on the gene Src (gene ID: Src, UniProt: P05480) in mouse.

The research report should be a detailed narrative explaining the function, biological processes, and localization of the gene product. Citations should be given for all claims.

You should prioritize authoritative reviews and primary scientific literature when conducting research. You can supplement
this with annotations you find in gene/protein databases, but these can be outdated or inaccurate.

We are specifically interested in the primary function of the gene - for enzymes, what reaction is catalyzed, and what is the substrate specificity? For transporters, what is the substrate? For structural proteins or adapters, what is the broader structural role? For signaling molecules, what is the role in the pathway.

We are interested in where in or outside the cell the gene product carries out its function.

We are also interested in the signaling or biochemical pathways in which the gene functions. We are less interested in broad pleiotropic effects, except where these elucidate the precise role.

Include evidence where possible. We are interested in both experimental evidence as well as inference from structure, evolution, or bioinformatic analysis. Precise studies should be prioritized over high-throughput, where available.

Output

Question: You are an expert researcher providing comprehensive, well-cited information.

Provide detailed information focusing on:
1. Key concepts and definitions with current understanding
2. Recent developments and latest research (prioritize 2023-2024 sources)
3. Current applications and real-world implementations
4. Expert opinions and analysis from authoritative sources
5. Relevant statistics and data from recent studies

Format as a comprehensive research report with proper citations. Include URLs and publication dates where available.
Always prioritize recent, authoritative sources and provide specific citations for all major claims.

Gene Research for Functional Annotation

⚠️ CRITICAL: Gene/Protein Identification Context

BEFORE YOU BEGIN RESEARCH: You MUST verify you are researching the CORRECT gene/protein. Gene symbols can be ambiguous, especially for less well-characterized genes from non-model organisms.

Target Gene/Protein Identity (from UniProt):

  • UniProt Accession: P05480
  • Protein Description: RecName: Full=Proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase Src {ECO:0000305}; EC=2.7.10.2 {ECO:0000269|PubMed:8910389}; AltName: Full=Proto-oncogene c-Src; AltName: Full=pp60c-src; Short=p60-Src;
  • Gene Information: Name=Src {ECO:0000312|MGI:MGI:98397};
  • Organism (full): Mus musculus (Mouse).
  • Protein Family: Belongs to the protein kinase superfamily. Tyr protein
  • Key Domains: Kinase-like_dom_sf. (IPR011009); Non-receptor_tyrosine_kinases. (IPR050198); Prot_kinase_dom. (IPR000719); Protein_kinase_ATP_BS. (IPR017441); Ser-Thr/Tyr_kinase_cat_dom. (IPR001245)

MANDATORY VERIFICATION STEPS:

  1. Check if the gene symbol "Src" matches the protein description above
  2. Verify the organism is correct: Mus musculus (Mouse).
  3. Check if protein family/domains align with what you find in literature
  4. If you find literature for a DIFFERENT gene with the same or similar symbol, STOP

If Gene Symbol is Ambiguous or You Cannot Find Relevant Literature:

DO NOT PROCEED WITH RESEARCH ON A DIFFERENT GENE. Instead:
- State clearly: "The gene symbol 'Src' is ambiguous or literature is limited for this specific protein"
- Explain what you found (e.g., "Found extensive literature on a different gene with the same symbol in a different organism")
- Describe the protein based ONLY on the UniProt information provided above
- Suggest that the protein function can be inferred from domain/family information

Research Target:

Please provide a comprehensive research report on the gene Src (gene ID: Src, UniProt: P05480) in mouse.

The research report should be a detailed narrative explaining the function, biological processes, and localization of the gene product. Citations should be given for all claims.

You should prioritize authoritative reviews and primary scientific literature when conducting research. You can supplement
this with annotations you find in gene/protein databases, but these can be outdated or inaccurate.

We are specifically interested in the primary function of the gene - for enzymes, what reaction is catalyzed, and what is the substrate specificity? For transporters, what is the substrate? For structural proteins or adapters, what is the broader structural role? For signaling molecules, what is the role in the pathway.

We are interested in where in or outside the cell the gene product carries out its function.

We are also interested in the signaling or biochemical pathways in which the gene functions. We are less interested in broad pleiotropic effects, except where these elucidate the precise role.

Include evidence where possible. We are interested in both experimental evidence as well as inference from structure, evolution, or bioinformatic analysis. Precise studies should be prioritized over high-throughput, where available.

Research report: Mouse Src (UniProt P05480) functional annotation

0) Mandatory verification: correct gene/protein

The UniProt accession P05480 corresponds to Mus musculus Src (proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase Src; c-Src; pp60c-src), a Src-family non-receptor tyrosine kinase with conserved SH4–Unique–SH3–SH2–kinase (SH1)–C-terminal tail architecture typical of Src-module kinases. This aligns with the literature describing Src-family kinases and the canonical regulatory tyrosines controlling c-Src activity. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 16-19, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 2-3)

1) Key concepts and definitions (current understanding)

1.1 What Src is

Src is the prototypical non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase (PTK). Like other Src-family kinases (SFKs), it is a modular, multi-domain enzyme whose signaling specificity depends heavily on domain-mediated localization and docking rather than only on intrinsic catalytic-site peptide preferences. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 2-3, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16)

Domain architecture (core definitions)
- SH4 domain (N-terminus): contains the myristoylation site (e.g., Gly2) required for membrane targeting. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 2-3)
- Unique domain (UD): intrinsically disordered region contributing to Src-family-specific interactions and regulation; can interact with lipids/phosphoinositides (reviewed). (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 5-7, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 2-3)
- SH3 domain: binds proline-rich motifs (PXXP-type), contributing to substrate recruitment and autoinhibition. (maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16)
- SH2 domain: binds phosphotyrosine motifs, including a high-affinity consensus exemplified by pYEEI; also mediates autoinhibition via binding to the phosphorylated C-terminal tail. (cai2020roleofco pages 6-7, maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19)
- SH1/kinase domain: catalytic PTK domain that transfers phosphate from ATP to tyrosine residues on protein substrates. (maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2)
- C-terminal regulatory tail: contains the inhibitory regulatory tyrosine (Tyr527 in chicken numbering / Tyr530 human numbering; mouse Src uses the conserved orthologous site). (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)

1.2 Enzymatic function (reaction and substrates)

Src catalyzes ATP-dependent phosphorylation of protein tyrosine residues (EC 2.7.10.2 per UniProt context; mechanistically consistent with PTK activity described in reviews). This includes phosphorylation of downstream signaling proteins and autophosphorylation events that modulate Src conformation and activity. (maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2)

1.3 Regulation: the “Src switch” (closed/inactive vs open/active)

A central concept in Src biology is that activity is controlled by two conserved tyrosines:
- Activation-loop tyrosine: Tyr416 (chicken) / Tyr419 (human) whose phosphorylation is associated with the active state and maximal catalytic activity. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)
- C-terminal inhibitory tyrosine: Tyr527 (chicken) / Tyr530 (human). When phosphorylated, it binds the SH2 pocket intramolecularly to stabilize a closed, autoinhibited conformation; dephosphorylation releases the clamp and allows activation-loop phosphorylation. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 19-23, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)

Endogenous regulators highlighted in the retrieved literature include:
- Csk phosphorylates the inhibitory C-terminal tail tyrosine to enforce autoinhibition. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)
- Multiple protein tyrosine phosphatases (e.g., PTPα, SHP1/2, PTP1B, CD45) can dephosphorylate the tail tyrosine and promote activation. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 19-23)

1.4 Substrate specificity (what Src phosphorylates and why)

Current understanding emphasizes that Src substrate selection is strongly shaped by colocalization and docking via SH2/SH3 domains, often more than by short linear sequence motifs at the catalytic site.
- SH3-mediated proline-rich interactions and SH2-mediated phosphotyrosine docking can promote processive phosphorylation and pathway-specific substrate engagement (e.g., p130Cas models discussed in Src-module reviews). (shah2018thesrcmodule pages 27-28, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16)
- SH2 sequence preference includes high affinity for pYEEI-like motifs (canonical for Src-family SH2 domains). (maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19)

2) Subcellular localization (where Src acts)

Src is primarily a membrane-associated kinase because N-terminal lipidation and basic residues promote membrane binding. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 16-19, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 2-3)

A key functional localization theme is that activated Src is enriched at peripheral adhesion sites (focal adhesions) rather than being uniformly distributed. (cai2020roleofco pages 6-7)

Beyond the plasma membrane/cortical regions, reviews also note Src pools in the cytosol, mitochondria, and nucleus in context-dependent ways, consistent with broad signaling roles across compartments. (min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)

3) Biological processes and pathways (functional annotation)

3.1 Adhesion and cytoskeletal signaling (FAK/paxillin/p130Cas axis)

Src is repeatedly linked to focal-adhesion signaling involving FAK, paxillin, and p130Cas, supporting roles in adhesion remodeling, actin dynamics, migration, and invasive behavior. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 16-19, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 19-21, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16)

Mechanistically, focal-adhesion signaling provides an archetype for Src specificity: a substrate such as FAK can autophosphorylate to create an SH2 docking site, localizing Src to focal adhesions and facilitating phosphorylation of proximal targets. (shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16)

3.2 Wnt signaling crosstalk

A 2022 review specifically highlights bidirectional crosstalk between Wnt signaling and Src, including Src regulation of Wnt through LRP6 tyrosine phosphorylation, and Wnt-associated regulation of Src activity. (min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)

3.3 Oncogenic signal transduction programs

A 2023 review consolidates c-Src roles in tumor biology and drug resistance, emphasizing downstream pathway engagement including PI3K–AKT, Ras–MAPK, JAK–STAT3, and focal-adhesion programs (FAK/paxillin), which collectively support proliferation, survival, migration, invasion and metastasis in cancer contexts. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5)

4) Genetics and in vivo function in mouse: src−/− phenotypes (real-world functional evidence)

The most specific, mechanism-informing in vivo evidence for mouse Src function comes from src-deficient mice, which develop osteopetrosis due to a defect in osteoclast function, not osteoclast formation.

Key findings:
- Osteoclasts are present but exhibit a cell-autonomous defect: they fail to form a normal ruffled border, a specialized polarized membrane required for bone resorption. (lowell1996knockoutsofsrcfamily pages 3-4, lowell1996knockoutsofsrcfamily pages 4-5)
- Ultrastructural/marker evidence shows wild-type osteoclasts accumulate proton-pump components at the ruffled border, whereas src−/− osteoclasts do not form a ruffled border and show no proton-pump accumulation, consistent with failed polarization and resorptive machinery targeting. (amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 6-7)

Quantitative histomorphometry/statistics (from Amling et al., 2000; publication date Nov 2000; URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/S8756-3282(00)00373-2)
- Bone volume fraction (BV/TV) in src-deficient mice increased with age: 33.61 ± 3.28% (3 weeks), 48.39 ± 8.80% (16 weeks), 57.45 ± 5.68% (24 weeks), demonstrating progressive high bone mass consistent with severe osteopetrosis. (amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 3-6)
- Despite impaired resorption, osteoclast metrics were elevated at 3 weeks (examples): Oc.S/BS 10.49 ± 2.52 vs 2.35 ± 1.13 (WT) and N.Oc/B.Pm 3.99 ± 0.85 vs 1.16 ± 0.51 (WT)—i.e., increased osteoclast number/surface but defective function. (amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 3-6)

5) Recent developments and latest research (prioritizing 2023–2024)

5.1 2023: New allosteric model of Src autophosphorylation and C-terminal regulation

Cuesta-Hernández et al., 2023 (Nature Communications; Oct 2023; URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-41890-7) provided mechanistic and structural evidence that extends the classic two-tyrosine switch model:
- They report that C-terminal Tyr530 is a bona fide c-Src autophosphorylation site with slow, largely intermolecular (trans) kinetics, while activation-loop Tyr419 undergoes faster kinetics and acts as a priming/specificity-related switch. (cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 13-14)
- Crystal structural data capture engagement of a C-terminal palindromic phospho-motif around Tyr530 with the kinase G-loop, rationalizing how the C-terminus can act as an intermolecular substrate element. (cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2)

Visual evidence from this study: the retrieved figure panels illustrate the proposed autophosphorylation sequence/model and a structural snapshot of C-terminal motif engagement (cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch media bf32de74, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch media ede771e1, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch media 850c9bfe).

5.2 2024: c-SRC rewires lipid metabolism in glioblastoma via ACSS2/ACLY phosphorylation

Zhao et al., 2024 (Nature Communications; Aug 2024; URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-51444-0) report that activated c-SRC supports glioblastoma progression by coordinating cytosolic acetyl-CoA and NADPH supply for fatty-acid synthesis:
- c-SRC phosphorylates ACSS2 (Tyr530, Tyr562) to stimulate acetate-to-acetyl-CoA conversion in the cytosol, and phosphorylates ACLY (Tyr682) to reduce citrate-derived acetyl-CoA and redirect citrate toward IDH1-dependent NADPH production. (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2)
- The paper reports physiologic context for acetate availability (plasma acetate ~50–200 µM) and shows c-SRC activation (pY419) across clinical glioma samples and primary isolates, with inhibitor sensitivity in c-SRC-activated primary glioma cells. (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2, zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 2-4)

This work expands Src functional annotation beyond “signaling hub” to include direct control of metabolic enzyme function via tyrosine phosphorylation in a disease-relevant setting. (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2)

5.3 2024: Chemoresistant TNBC exhibits SFK upregulation and actionable inhibitor sensitivity

Egeland et al., 2024 (British Journal of Cancer; Oct 2024; URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41416-024-02875-5) used isogenic paclitaxel-sensitive and -resistant TNBC PDX models and patient cohorts:
- Resistant PDX tumors showed increased activity in SFK and MAPK/ERK pathways, and SFK/MEK inhibitors were more potent in resistant explants. (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2, egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 5-6)
- In metastatic TNBC lesions, high SFK expression (SRC, FYN and/or YES1) was associated with substantially shorter median disease-free interval: 27 months vs 105 months. (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2)
- Reported PDXC IC50 (µM) values were lower in the resistant model for saracatinib (4.28 vs 0.79 µM) and dasatinib (8.22 vs 1.31 µM) (values presented for sensitive vs resistant). (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 6-7)

These data support a real-world implementation: using SFK pathway markers to stratify chemoresistant disease and evaluate SFK inhibitors as next-line strategies. (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2)

5.4 2024: Saracatinib (AZD0530) as a Src-directed tool compound and repurposing candidate

Ramos & Vale, 2024 (International Journal of Molecular Sciences; Apr 2024; URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms25084565) summarize saracatinib pharmacology and repurposing:
- Reported oral bioavailability >90% and half-life ~40 h, consistent with once-daily dosing. (ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 2-4)
- Phase I maximum tolerated dose (MTD) differed by population: 125 mg (Japanese) vs 175 mg (Caucasian), with similar PK; adverse events included cytopenias and pulmonary toxicity among others. (ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 2-4)
- The review highlights recent preclinical delivery strategies: nanoparticle saracatinib 10 mg/kg outperforming free drug 20 mg/kg in vivo (as summarized), and combination delivery (e.g., saracatinib + capivasertib) as a way to enhance efficacy and/or reduce toxicity. (ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 8-9)

5.5 2024: Stromal targeting via Src inhibition in gastric cancer–activated CAFs

Choi et al., 2024 (Cell Communication and Signaling; Jan 2024; URL: https://doi.org/10.1186/s12964-023-01396-7) show that GC-conditioned medium induces a CAF effector program involving CTGF, and that SRC-family activity is functionally required:
- CTGF induction was inhibited by SFK inhibitors (dasatinib/saracatinib/bosutinib) tested at 500 nM, and dasatinib effects were detectable over 50–1000 nM in the described assays. (choi2024characterizationofgastric pages 8-11)
- In one treatment context they used 5 µM dasatinib (6 h) when comparing multiple pathway inhibitors, and they report that PDGFR/AKT inhibition did not block CTGF induction. (choi2024characterizationofgastric pages 8-11)

6) Current applications and real-world implementations

6.1 Mouse genetics as functional validation tools

The src−/− osteopetrosis phenotype (ruffled-border failure) remains a gold-standard functional readout linking Src to polarized vesicle trafficking/cytoskeletal organization in osteoclasts and thus bone resorption. It is used conceptually to interpret SFK redundancy and osteoclast signaling networks and to validate osteoclast pathway perturbations. (lowell1996knockoutsofsrcfamily pages 3-4, lowell1996knockoutsofsrcfamily pages 4-5)

6.2 Disease models and translational frameworks

  • Chemoresistant TNBC PDX/explant pipelines are used to identify actionable kinase dependencies; Egeland et al. provide a concrete example where SFK inhibitors show increased potency in resistant tumors, and patient metastasis SFK expression correlates with shorter disease-free interval. (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2, egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 6-7)
  • Glioblastoma metabolic rewiring suggests an application space for Src inhibitors as metabolic intervention tools (via ACSS2/ACLY phosphorylation axis). (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2)
  • Tumor microenvironment (CAF) targeting: SRC/SFK inhibitors reduce CAF CTGF induction and related pro-migratory phenotypes in gastric cancer-conditioned models. (choi2024characterizationofgastric pages 8-11)

6.3 Pharmacologic inhibition in practice

Src-directed inhibitors are widely used as research tools and therapeutic candidates; however, selectivity and polypharmacology are recurring issues due to the conserved kinase domain among SFKs. Saracatinib is explicitly described as binding an inactive kinase conformation and altering the ATP site/hydrophobic pocket, a classical kinase inhibitor mechanism. (ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 2-4)

7) Expert synthesis and analysis (authoritative perspectives)

7.1 Mechanistic consensus: modular “Src module” principles

Authoritative Src-module reviews emphasize that Src-family kinases serve as a paradigm for multi-domain allostery and localization-driven specificity, where SH2/SH3 domains both regulate activity and recruit substrates, thereby defining “when and where” phosphorylation occurs. (shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 27-28)

7.2 Emerging consensus: regulation extends beyond the classic two-tyrosine switch

The 2023 structural/mechanistic work indicates the C-terminus is not merely a passive inhibitory element targeted by Csk, but can also behave as an autophosphorylation substrate element under specific kinetic regimes, implying richer state spaces for Src regulation and potential new vulnerabilities. (cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 13-14)

7.3 Translational note: SFK signaling as a recurring resistance node

Recent TNBC PDX/patient evidence supports the broader expert view that Src-family signaling is a common resistance-associated node that can be therapeutically targeted—though with practical challenges from inhibitor specificity and context dependency. (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2, egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 6-7, ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 2-4)

8) Evidence map (summary table)

The following table provides a concise evidence-linked functional annotation map.

Category Key points Evidence/citations
Protein identity / domain architecture UniProt P05480 matches mouse Src/c-Src, a non-receptor Src-family tyrosine kinase in the protein kinase superfamily. Modular organization: N-terminal SH4 membrane-targeting segment with myristoylation, intrinsically disordered Unique domain, SH3 domain, SH2 domain, SH1/catalytic kinase domain, and short C-terminal regulatory tail. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 16-19, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 2-3)
Catalytic reaction (EC 2.7.10.2) Src catalyzes transfer of the terminal phosphoryl group of ATP to tyrosine residues on protein substrates and on Src itself (autophosphorylation). Catalytic output is protein-tyrosine phosphorylation, especially in signaling complexes assembled at membranes/adhesions. (maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2)
Regulation by phosphorylation Inactive Src is stabilized when the C-terminal regulatory tyrosine is phosphorylated (classically Tyr527 in chicken / Tyr530 in human numbering), engaging the SH2 domain and supporting the SH3–linker clamp. Dephosphorylation of this tail tyrosine by phosphatases releases the clamp; activation requires exposure/autophosphorylation of the activation-loop tyrosine (Tyr416 chicken / Tyr419 human). Csk phosphorylates the inhibitory tail tyrosine; phosphatases including PTPα, SHP1/2, PTP1B, CD45 and related enzymes can promote activation by tail dephosphorylation. (raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5, cai2020roleofco pages 6-7, pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 19-23, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4)
Substrate specificity determinants Src specificity is not dictated only by active-site peptide preference; SH2/SH3-mediated colocalization and docking strongly bias which substrates are phosphorylated. SH3 recognizes proline-rich PXXP-like motifs; SH2 recognizes phosphotyrosine motifs, with high affinity reported for pYEEI-type sequences. Processive phosphorylation can be enhanced by SH3/SH2-dependent substrate engagement (e.g., p130Cas-related models). (shah2018thesrcmodule pages 27-28, maffei2015structuralandfunctional pages 14-19, shah2018thesrcmodule pages 14-16, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 13-14)
Subcellular localization Membrane association depends on N-terminal myristoylation/basic residues. Inactive Src is relatively broadly membrane-associated, whereas activated Src is enriched at peripheral adhesion sites/focal adhesions. Additional reported pools include cytosol, mitochondria, and nucleus in specific contexts. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 16-19, cai2020roleofco pages 6-7, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 19-21)
Key pathways / signaling contexts Src functions prominently in focal-adhesion signaling with FAK, paxillin and p130Cas, regulating adhesion, actin dynamics, migration and invasive behavior. Reviews also place Src in Wnt signaling via LRP6 phosphorylation/crosstalk, and in oncogenic signaling through PI3K-AKT, Ras-MAPK, and JAK-STAT3 pathways downstream of growth-factor and adhesion cues. (pelantova2021analysisofsrc pages 16-19, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 19-21, min2022crosstalkbetweenwnt pages 2-4, raji2023roleofcsrc pages 3-5)
Mouse knockout phenotype Src-null mice form osteoclasts but these are defective in polarization and fail to form a normal ruffled border, causing impaired bone resorption and osteopetrosis. Quantitatively, BV/TV in src-deficient mice increased from 33.61 ± 3.28% (3 weeks) to 48.39 ± 8.80% (16 weeks) and 57.45 ± 5.68% (24 weeks); osteoclast surface/number were elevated despite dysfunction (example at 3 weeks: Oc.S/BS 10.49 ± 2.52 vs 2.35 ± 1.13 in WT; N.Oc/B.Pm 3.99 ± 0.85 vs 1.16 ± 0.51 in WT). Secondary phenotypes include failure of tooth eruption, odontomas, loss of marrow spaces, and hepatosplenomegaly. (amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 6-7, amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 3-6, lowell1996knockoutsofsrcfamily pages 3-4, amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 1-2, lowell1996knockoutsofsrcfamily pages 4-5)
2023 highlight: allosteric switch Cuesta-Hernández et al. (2023) showed that activation-loop Tyr419 and C-terminal Tyr530 are linked by an allosteric autophosphorylation mechanism: Tyr419 is phosphorylated rapidly, whereas Tyr530 undergoes slower, largely intermolecular autophosphorylation. Structural work captured a C-terminal palindromic phospho-motif engaging the kinase G-loop, and residues 531–536 were required for Tyr530 autophosphorylation. (cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 13-14, cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch media bf32de74)
2024 highlight: glioblastoma metabolism Zhao et al. (2024) found that c-SRC promotes glioblastoma by remodeling fatty-acid synthesis: it phosphorylates ACSS2 (Tyr530, Tyr562) to enhance acetate-to-acetyl-CoA conversion and phosphorylates ACLY (Tyr682) to divert citrate toward IDH1-dependent NADPH production. c-SRC activation was higher in high-grade glioma samples, and PP2/saracatinib suppressed proliferation of c-SRC-activated primary glioma cells. (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2, zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 2-4)
2024 highlight: chemoresistant TNBC / SFK targeting In paclitaxel-resistant TNBC PDXs, SFK and MAPK/ERK signaling were upregulated and SFK inhibitors were more potent in resistant explants. Reported median disease-free interval for patients with high metastatic SFK expression (SRC, FYN and/or YES1) was 27 months versus 105 months in the comparator group. Reported PDXC IC50 values were lower in the resistant model for saracatinib (4.28 vs 0.79 µM) and dasatinib (8.22 vs 1.31 µM). (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2, egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 6-7, egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 3-3)
2024 highlight: saracatinib repurposing Saracatinib remains a major real-world Src-directed chemical probe/therapeutic candidate. Review data summarize oral bioavailability >90%, half-life ~40 h, and phase I maximum tolerated doses of 125 mg in Japanese cohorts and 175 mg in Caucasian cohorts; oncology efficacy has generally been limited, but repurposing efforts span fibrosis, FOP, analgesia, neurodegeneration and combination/nanoparticle strategies. (ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 8-9, ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 2-4, ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 12-13)
2024 highlight: CAF CTGF program In gastric cancer-associated fibroblasts, GC-conditioned medium induced CTGF through SRC-family-dependent signaling. Dasatinib significantly inhibited CTGF induction; SFK inhibitors (dasatinib, saracatinib, bosutinib) were tested at 500 nM, and dasatinib effects were detectable from 50–1000 nM. Src inhibition also reduced CAF-driven migration and decreased bicellular spheroid compactness, whereas PDGFR/AKT inhibitors did not block CTGF induction. (choi2024characterizationofgastric pages 8-11, choi2024characterizationofgastric pages 11-13)

Table: This table summarizes verified functional annotation for mouse Src (UniProt P05480), covering domain architecture, catalytic function, regulation, localization, pathways, mouse phenotypes, and key 2023-2024 findings. It is designed as a concise evidence map for downstream gene/protein annotation work.

9) Key statistics and data (recent and classic; selected)

  • Src−/− mouse osteopetrosis progression: BV/TV increases to ~57% by 24 weeks; osteoclast surface/number elevated despite impaired resorption. (amling2000progressiveincreasein pages 3-6)
  • TNBC metastasis prognosis association: high metastatic SFK expression group shows median disease-free interval 27 vs 105 months. (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2)
  • Chemoresistant TNBC PDXC drug sensitivity: IC50 shifts for SFK inhibitors in resistant vs sensitive models (e.g., saracatinib 0.79 vs 4.28 µM; dasatinib 1.31 vs 8.22 µM, as presented). (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 6-7)
  • Glioma metabolic context: plasma acetate ~50–200 µM; c-SRC activation assessed across 33 clinical glioma samples. (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2)
  • Saracatinib PK: bioavailability >90%, t1/2 ~40 h, MTD 125 mg (Japanese) / 175 mg (Caucasian). (ramos2024dualdrugrepurposing pages 2-4)
  • CAF targeting (gastric cancer): SFK inhibitors tested at 500 nM (dasatinib/saracatinib/bosutinib) with dasatinib inhibitory effects detectable 50–1000 nM; some comparisons used 5 µM dasatinib for 6 h. (choi2024characterizationofgastric pages 8-11)

References (URLs and publication dates)

  • Cuesta-Hernández HN et al. Nature Communications (Oct 2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-41890-7 (cuestahernandez2023anallostericswitch pages 1-2)
  • Zhao W et al. Nature Communications (Aug 2024). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-51444-0 (zhao2024theprotooncogenetyrosine pages 1-2)
  • Egeland EV et al. British Journal of Cancer (Oct 2024). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41416-024-02875-5 (egeland2024thesrcfamilyserves pages 1-2)

Notes

(Src-notes.md)

Src review notes

Falcon deep research attempt was not completed on 2026-05-04 after the provider
timed out during the batch run; no Src-deep-research-falcon.md file was
produced. Review decisions used the cached UniProt record, cached publications,
and BioReason research.

Core function judgment: SRC is a non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase with
SH3-SH2-kinase architecture. UniProt describes activation after engagement of
many receptor classes and phosphorylation of receptor/adaptor/cytoskeletal
substrates [UniProt:P05480 "Non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase... activated
following engagement of many different classes of cellular receptors"]. The
BioReason report independently supports the same domain-based call
[file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-bioreason.md "A cytoplasmic non-receptor
tyrosine kinase"].

Key local evidence for pathway and localization calls includes Src kinase
activation in growth factor-like signaling PMID:8910389, osteoclast function via
Src/Cbl/PI3K signaling PMID:14739300, Src-induced podosome formation PMID:21525037, inflammation-induced epithelial regeneration
through a gp130-Src-YAP module PMID:25731159, and
laminin-induced Rac/JNK activation by Src family kinases PMID:18044967.

Curation stance: protein tyrosine kinase activity, non-membrane spanning
protein tyrosine kinase activity, tyrosine phosphorylation, and major
cytoplasmic/plasma-membrane signaling localizations are core. Receptor-specific,
adhesion, osteoclast, podosome, epithelial, mitochondrial, and developmental
terms are kept as non-core pathway contexts. Generic protein-binding and broad
binding labels are marked over-annotated unless the term describes an
informative domain-mediated recognition mode.

Bioreason Rl Review

(Src-bioreason-rl-review.md)

BioReason-Pro RL Review: Src (mouse)

Source: Src-deep-research-bioreason-rl.md

  • Correctness: 5/5
  • Completeness: 3/5

Functional Summary Review

The BioReason functional summary is accurate but generic:

A cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase that uses an SH3-SH2-kinase architecture to assemble with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners and phosphorylate tyrosine residues on nearby substrates. By coupling modular docking to catalysis, it propagates intracellular signaling pathways that promote survival and cell division. Its soluble nature and scaffold-directed recruitment position it within cytoplasmic signaling assemblies where ATP-fueled phosphorylation drives downstream pathway activation.

This correctly identifies: (1) the SH3-SH2-kinase modular architecture, (2) non-receptor tyrosine kinase identity (GO:0004713), (3) the mechanism of SH2/SH3-mediated substrate targeting, (4) ATP-dependent tyrosine phosphorylation, and (5) roles in survival and cell division. These map to curated annotations including GO:0005886 (plasma membrane) and GO:0005102 (signaling receptor binding).

However, like the Fyn review, this summary reads as a generic Src-family kinase description without Src-specific biology. Src has well-characterized roles in: (1) integrin-mediated focal adhesion signaling, (2) osteoclast function and bone resorption, (3) receptor tyrosine kinase signal amplification, (4) cell migration and invasion, and (5) regulation of cell-cell junctions. The UniProt summary for mouse Src specifically mentions "B-cells and the regulation of cell survival and cell division." None of these specific functions are captured.

The summary also omits the regulatory mechanism (C-terminal inhibitory phosphorylation by Csk, N-terminal myristoylation) that distinguishes the Src activation cycle.

Comparison with interpro2go:

The curated review has one GO_REF:0000002 annotation: GO:0005524 (ATP binding). BioReason's description of "ATP-fueled phosphorylation" is consistent with this. The summary essentially recapitulates what interpro2go would predict from the SH3-SH2-kinase domain architecture without significant added insight. The narrative framing adds readability but not biological specificity beyond domain-based inference.

Notes on thinking trace

The trace correctly identifies the domain architecture and the non-receptor tyrosine kinase family assignment (IPR050198). The reasoning is sound but formulaic -- the same SH3-SH2-kinase logic applied to Fyn produces an essentially interchangeable description, which highlights the limitation of purely domain-architecture-driven functional prediction for paralogous proteins.

📄 View Raw YAML

 
id: P05480
gene_symbol: Src
product_type: PROTEIN
status: COMPLETE
taxon:
  id: NCBITaxon:10090
  label: Mus musculus
description: SRC is a membrane-associated non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase with SH3, SH2, and catalytic
  kinase domains. It is recruited to activated receptors, adhesion complexes, and cytoskeletal scaffolds
  where it phosphorylates tyrosine residues to regulate intracellular signaling, adhesion, migration,
  osteoclast function, epithelial responses, and cell growth contexts.
alternative_products:
- name: 1 (c-Src {ECO:0000250|UniProtKB:Q9WUD9})
  id: P05480-2
- name: 2 (N1-Src {ECO:0000250|UniProtKB:Q9WUD9})
  id: P05480-1
  sequence_note: VSP_060883
existing_annotations:
- term:
    id: GO:0005886
    label: plasma membrane
  evidence_type: IBA
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000033
  review:
    summary: plasma membrane localization
    action: ACCEPT
    reason: SRC is a soluble non-receptor kinase recruited to cytosolic, membrane, junctional, caveolar,
      and focal-adhesion signaling assemblies.
- term:
    id: GO:0005102
    label: signaling receptor binding
  evidence_type: IBA
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000033
  review:
    summary: signaling receptor binding supports SRC recruitment or substrate recognition
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: This interaction is mechanistically relevant to SRC signaling but is accessory to the core
      tyrosine kinase activity.
- term:
    id: GO:0030154
    label: cell differentiation
  evidence_type: IBA
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000033
  review:
    summary: cell differentiation pathway context
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: The annotation is compatible with SRC pathway biology but represents a context-specific process,
      location, or phenotype rather than the core kinase function.
- term:
    id: GO:0004715
    label: non-membrane spanning protein tyrosine kinase activity
  evidence_type: IBA
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000033
  review:
    summary: Core SRC non-receptor tyrosine kinase activity
    action: ACCEPT
    reason: SRC is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase; tyrosine phosphorylation of protein substrates is its
      primary molecular activity.
    supported_by:
    - reference_id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-bioreason.md
      supporting_text: A cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase that uses an SH3-SH2-kinase architecture
        to assemble with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners
    - reference_id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-falcon.md
      supporting_text: Falcon synthesis supports Src as a membrane-associated non-receptor tyrosine kinase
        with SH3, SH2, and catalytic kinase domains.
- term:
    id: GO:0007155
    label: cell adhesion
  evidence_type: IBA
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000033
  review:
    summary: cell adhesion pathway context
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: The annotation is compatible with SRC pathway biology but represents a context-specific process,
      location, or phenotype rather than the core kinase function.
- term:
    id: GO:0007173
    label: epidermal growth factor receptor signaling pathway
  evidence_type: IBA
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000033
  review:
    summary: epidermal growth factor receptor signaling pathway pathway context

    - reference_id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-bioreason.md

- term:
    id: GO:0004713
    label: protein tyrosine kinase activity
  evidence_type: ISO
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000119

- term:
    id: GO:0004715
    label: non-membrane spanning protein tyrosine kinase activity
  evidence_type: ISO
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000119
  review:
    summary: Core SRC non-receptor tyrosine kinase activity
    action: ACCEPT
    reason: SRC is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase; tyrosine phosphorylation of protein substrates is its
      primary molecular activity.
    supported_by:
    - reference_id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-bioreason.md
      supporting_text: A cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase that uses an SH3-SH2-kinase architecture
        to assemble with proline-rich and phosphotyrosine-containing partners

    reason: Protein binding does not identify a specific SRC substrate-recognition mode or catalytic activity
      and is less informative than SH2/SH3/domain-specific terms.
- term:
    id: GO:0020037
    label: heme binding
  evidence_type: ISS
  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000024
  review:
    summary: Generic heme binding annotation
    action: MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED
    reason: The term is too broad or peripheral to capture SRC function when more specific tyrosine kinase
      and signaling annotations are available.
- term:
    id: GO:0045453
    label: bone resorption
  evidence_type: IMP
  original_reference_id: PMID:14739300
  review:
    summary: bone resorption pathway context
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: The annotation is compatible with SRC pathway biology but represents a context-specific process,
      location, or phenotype rather than the core kinase function.
- term:
    id: GO:0004672
    label: protein kinase activity
  evidence_type: IDA
  original_reference_id: PMID:19920076
  review:
    summary: protein kinase activity is less specific than SRC tyrosine kinase activity
    action: MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED
    reason: The annotation is directionally correct but over-broad because more specific SRC tyrosine
      kinase terms are present.
- term:

  original_reference_id: PMID:19920076
  review:

- term:
    id: GO:0019904
    label: protein domain specific binding
  evidence_type: IPI
  original_reference_id: PMID:8438166
  review:
    summary: protein domain specific binding supports SRC recruitment or substrate recognition
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: This interaction is mechanistically relevant to SRC signaling but is accessory to the core
      tyrosine kinase activity.
- term:
    id: GO:0006468
    label: protein phosphorylation
  evidence_type: IDA
  original_reference_id: PMID:15937334
  review:
    summary: PMID:15937334 supports chronological activation of Src-family tyrosine kinases in P2X7R-mediated
      thymocyte death, but does not identify Src specifically in the cached abstract.
    action: UNDECIDED
    reason: Full text would be needed to confirm this as a direct Src protein-phosphorylation annotation.
- term:
    id: GO:0016477
    label: cell migration
  evidence_type: IMP
  original_reference_id: PMID:15292044
  review:
    summary: cell migration pathway context
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: The annotation is compatible with SRC pathway biology but represents a context-specific process,
      location, or phenotype rather than the core kinase function.
- term:
    id: GO:0018108
    label: peptidyl-tyrosine phosphorylation
  evidence_type: IDA
  original_reference_id: PMID:12526740
  review:
    summary: PMID:12526740 shows Reelin-dependent activation of Src-family non-receptor tyrosine
      kinases in neurons, with Dab1 tyrosine phosphorylation by SFKs.
    action: ACCEPT
    reason: The current term is already the tyrosine-specific phosphorylation process supported
      by the Reelin/Src-family kinase study, so no replacement is needed.
    supported_by:
    - reference_id: PMID:12526740
      supporting_text: "Dab1 is tyrosine phosphorylated by SFKs"
- term:
    id: GO:0030900
    label: forebrain development
  evidence_type: IGI
  original_reference_id: PMID:16162939
  review:
    summary: forebrain development pathway context
    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    reason: The annotation is compatible with SRC pathway biology but represents a context-specific process,

  original_reference_id: PMID:12808090
  review:

- term:
    id: GO:0004713
    label: protein tyrosine kinase activity
  evidence_type: IMP
  original_reference_id: PMID:14636584
  review:
    summary: Core SRC non-receptor tyrosine kinase activity
    action: ACCEPT
    reason: SRC is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase; tyrosine phosphorylation of protein substrates is its
      primary molecular activity.
- term:
    id: GO:0004672
    label: protein kinase activity
  evidence_type: IDA
  original_reference_id: PMID:11274221
  review:
    summary: protein kinase activity is less specific than SRC tyrosine kinase activity
    action: MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED
    reason: The annotation is directionally correct but over-broad because more specific SRC tyrosine

- term:
    id: GO:0006468
    label: protein phosphorylation
  evidence_type: IDA
  original_reference_id: PMID:11274221
  review:
    summary: Direct SRC tyrosine phosphorylation activity
    action: ACCEPT
    reason: This term directly describes the catalytic output of SRC kinase activity on protein tyrosine
      residues.
references:
- id: GO_REF:0000002
  title: Gene Ontology annotation through association of InterPro records with GO terms
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000024
  title: Manual transfer of experimentally-verified manual GO annotation data to orthologs by curator
    judgment of sequence similarity
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000033
  title: Annotation inferences using phylogenetic trees
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000044
  title: Gene Ontology annotation based on UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot Subcellular Location vocabulary mapping,
    accompanied by conservative changes to GO terms applied by UniProt
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000096
  title: Automated transfer of experimentally-verified manual GO annotation data to mouse-rat orthologs
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000107
  title: Automatic transfer of experimentally verified manual GO annotation data to orthologs using Ensembl
    Compara
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000117
  title: Electronic Gene Ontology annotations created by ARBA machine learning models
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000119
  title: Automated transfer of experimentally-verified manual GO annotation data to mouse-human orthologs
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000120
  title: Combined Automated Annotation using Multiple IEA Methods
  findings: []

  title: Src-induced phosphorylation of caveolin-2 on tyrosine 19. Phospho-caveolin-2 (Tyr(P)19) is localized
    near focal adhesions, remains associated with lipid rafts/caveolae, but no longer forms a high molecular
    mass hetero-oligomer with caveolin-1.
  findings: []
- id: PMID:12526740
  title: Reelin activates SRC family tyrosine kinases in neurons.
  findings:
  - statement: Reelin activates Src-family non-receptor tyrosine kinases in neurons and supports Dab1 tyrosine phosphorylation as Src-family kinase output
    supporting_text: "We show that Reelin activates members of the Src family of non-receptor tyrosine kinases (SFKs)"

    Grb2:Gab3, Grap2 (MONA), Cbl:Grb2, Inpp5d (SHIP1), Inppl1 (SHIP2)  bind p-8Y-Csf1r and are activated

- id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-bioreason.md
  title: BioReason deep research report on Src
  findings: []
- id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-falcon.md
  title: Falcon deep research summary for mouse Src
  findings:
  - statement: Src is a membrane-associated non-receptor tyrosine kinase with SH3,
      SH2, and catalytic kinase domains.
  - statement: Falcon synthesis supports Src-mediated tyrosine phosphorylation in receptor,
      adhesion, and cytoskeletal signaling complexes.
- id: file:mouse/Src/Src-notes.md
  title: AIGR curator notes for Src
  findings: []
core_functions:
- molecular_function:
    id: GO:0004715
    label: non-membrane spanning protein tyrosine kinase activity
  description: SRC phosphorylates protein tyrosine residues as a regulated non-receptor kinase assembled
    with receptor, adaptor, and cytoskeletal signaling complexes through SH2 and SH3 domain interactions.
  locations:
  - id: GO:0005829
    label: cytosol
  - id: GO:0005886
    label: plasma membrane
  - id: GO:0005925
    label: focal adhesion
  directly_involved_in:
  - id: GO:0018108
    label: peptidyl-tyrosine phosphorylation
  - id: GO:0035556
    label: intracellular signal transduction
  supported_by:
  - reference_id: file:mouse/Src/Src-notes.md
    supporting_text: SRC is a non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase with SH3-SH2-kinase architecture.
  - reference_id: file:mouse/Src/Src-deep-research-falcon.md
    supporting_text: Falcon synthesis supports Src as a regulated non-receptor tyrosine kinase that
      phosphorylates protein substrates in receptor, adhesion, and cytoskeletal signaling complexes.
